summary:
Sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum) was domesticated in New Guinea and nearby island regions of the southwestern Pacific from wild sugarcane ancestors (Saccharum robustum), with the initial domestication process estimated to have begun approximately 8000 to 6000 years ago. New Guinea is the primary center of sugarcane domestication, a fact consistent with the island's broader role as a center of agricultural innovation that also produced the earliest known horticulture at [[event:1456|Kuk Swamp]].

The wild ancestors of cultivated sugarcane were tall perennial grasses growing in tropical lowland and highland margins. Early cultivation likely began with communities selecting and propagating cane plants for their sweet juice and edible pith, used as a source of immediate energy. The propagation of sugarcane through stem cuttings rather than seeds allowed selection for superior plants to be maintained reliably across generations, a method of vegetative propagation that remains central to sugarcane cultivation today.

From its New Guinea origins, domesticated sugarcane spread westward through maritime Southeast Asia to South Asia, reaching India by approximately 2500 to 1500 BCE, and subsequently to the Near East and Mediterranean world. For most of its global history, sugar from cane was an expensive luxury available only to the wealthy; it was the plantation agriculture of the early modern period — particularly in the Caribbean and Brazil, relying on enslaved African labor — that transformed sugar into a mass commodity and shaped the modern world in profound and often devastating ways.
